Track both cyclical (period-related) and non-cyclical symptoms—think pain, fatigue, digestion, mood.
Use a diary, app, or printable template—whatever works for you. Consistency matters most.

outline.key_point: Think your pain is being overlooked? Here’s the checklist of questions every clinician should ask before diagnosing endometriosis.
Many UK women feel dismissed or misdiagnosed when seeking help for endometriosis symptoms.
You’re not alone—clinician experience and the right questions make a real difference in diagnosis.
Experienced clinicians can suspect endometriosis with 99.9% accuracy by asking the right questions before doing any scans.
Here’s what you should expect your GP or specialist to ask:

• Detailed description of your pain (location, timing, severity)
• Menstrual history and cycle patterns

• Other symptoms (fatigue, bowel or bladder issues)
• Impact on daily life and activities

• Family history of endometriosis
• Any previous treatments or investigations
If you haven’t been asked these, it’s OK to raise them yourself. Being prepared can help you get the care you deserve.
Save this checklist for your next appointment, and share it to help other women feel heard.
